explanations.9: This runs the 'uname -s' command and stores its output (like 'Darwin' for Mac or 'Linux') in the os_type variable.
explanations.12: The 'command -v' checks if a program exists, and '>/dev/null 2>&1' throws away any output so we only care about success or failure.
explanations.18: The '&&' means both conditions must fail for this to trigger — it's checking that neither 'fd' nor 'fdfind' exist on this system.
explanations.25: This checks if the operating system is macOS by comparing the os_type variable to 'Darwin', which is macOS's internal name.
explanations.27: The '-f' flag checks if a file exists; this file only exists on Debian-based Linux systems, so it's a way to detect that OS type.
explanations.48: This checks which fd variant is available (sometimes it's called 'fdfind' on some Linux systems instead of 'fd').
explanations.55: This sets visual_editor to the $EDITOR environment variable, but if that's not set, it defaults to 'code' (VS Code).
explanations.62: The backslash at the end of a line lets you continue a long command on the next line — it's just breaking up the command for readability.
explanations.64: The 'sed' command removes the '/.git' directory name from the path, leaving just the project folder name.
explanations.74: The '%' operator removes the matching suffix from the end of the string — so this strips off any trailing '/.git' that might still be there.
explanations.85: The '-k 1' tells 'read' to wait for exactly 1 keypress instead of waiting for Enter, so the user gets immediate feedback.
explanations.88: This checks if REPLY is either a newline character (when the user just presses Enter) or the letters Y or y.
